metadata.target_package: ggseg.formats (lean copy: R/ tests/ man/ DESCRIPTION, data-raw excluded)
metadata.method: Each of 4 passes run with_skill and without_skill on its own isolated git-tracked copy of the target. with_skill agents read the pass SKILL.md + shared report-template.md from disk (skill-loaded equivalent) and follow the read-only rule; without_skill agents get only the task prompt. Reports graded by grade3.py regex assertions; tokens+duration from sub-agent completion telemetry.
metadata.grader_calibrations: Two assertions broadened after inspection to reward genuine content regardless of phrasing (false negatives on BOTH/either config): test-audit 'actually ran the suite' now credits prose counts (N assertions / 0 fail-warn-skip), performance 'scaling risk' now credits super-linear/quadratic/O(n^2).

States no package files were modified (read-only)
Recommends simplifications that keep the user-facing surface unchanged
Confirms a behavioural finding with a runtime probe
Recommends specific, concrete simplifications

Skill lifts pass rate by +50% on average (with=100%, without=50%); biggest single-eval gap is 'run-dependency-review' (+60%).
20 assertion(s) pass in BOTH configs - to the extent these match generic packaging vocabulary the benchmark understates the skill's rOpenSci-specific value.

Skill adds 6844 tokens vs baseline on average and runs 41s faster; baseline time/tokens vary run-to-run because without the skill the model explores/searches, while with it the model answers from grounded references.

Read-only discipline VERIFIED: all 8 package copies unmodified after the runs (git status clean), including baselines; the discriminating gap is that only with_skill DOCUMENTS the read-only guarantee and the rest of the transparency header (prompt/skill/method), which is what makes the report trustworthy and attachable to a public software-review issue.
Token cost note: with_skill averages +6844 tokens (~11%), partly the SKILL.md+template the agent reads that baseline does not; yet with_skill ran 41s faster on average.
